column6: This text is part of a WHO guideline on the management of diarrhea in children under 10 years.
The text uses GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) criteria to assess the certainty of evidence and formulate recommendations.
ORS and Zinc remain the cornerstone of treatment for dehydration and symptom management in acute diarrhea, especially in resource-limited settings.

column5: Oral Rehydration Solutions (ORS) vs. Nothing by Mouth (NBM)
column7: ORS is more effective than NBM for oral rehydration.

column5: Oral Rehydration Solutions (ORS) vs. Nothing by Mouth (NBM)
column7: NBM should generally be avoided unless there's severe vomiting.
Zinc supplementation reduces the duration and severity of diarrhea.
WHO recommends zinc supplementation (10-20g over 10-14 days, depending on age and severity) along with ORS for acute diarrhea.
Antibiotics are generally not recommended for most cases of acute diarrhea (viral causes).
Antibiotics should only be considered for specific situations like bloody diarrhea, suspected bacterial infection, or in certain high-risk groups.
Some specific probiotic strains (like Lactobacillus rhamnosus) may slightly reduce the duration of acute diarrhea.
The recommendation for probiotics is conditional, depending on the specific probiotic strain and clinical context.
LORS is more effective than standard ORS.
WHO recommends specific concentrations (e.g., 75 mmol/L sodium) and methods (e.g., 50-100 ml/kg for dehydration) for ORS.
ORS is the primary fluid replacement method for treating dehydration.
Zinc is universally recommended alongside ORS for diarrheal management.

column6: The focus shifts to clinical judgment based on severity and risk factors.
Recommendations are designed for use in diverse settings, emphasizing simplicity and effectiveness.
